title: Discovery Call Practice
description: Script a complete discovery call for a specific product and buyer persona, then role-play it with a partner. Practise open-ended questioning, active listening, pain quantification, and next-step setting. Record yourself if possible.
whyItMatters: You can study discovery frameworks all day, but the skill only develops through practice. The first few discovery calls every rep does are awkward — they ask leading questions, talk too much, forget to quantify pain, and end without next steps. This project gets the awkward reps out of the way in a safe environment so your real calls are sharper.
skillsLearned: BANT and MEDDIC qualification in practice

steps.title: Choose Your Scenario
steps.description: Select a product (real or hypothetical) and a buyer persona. Define the buyer's company, role, current situation, pain points (that they may or may not reveal easily), budget range, decision process, and timeline. Write a one-page scenario brief.
steps.tips: Make the scenario realistic. Include details that the buyer wouldn't volunteer unless asked — like a competitor evaluation in progress, or a sceptical CFO who needs convincing. This makes the role-play more valuable.

steps.title: Script Your Call
steps.description: Write a full discovery call script following the structure: Opening and agenda-setting (2 min) → Situational questions (5 min) → Pain discovery questions (10 min) → Impact quantification (5 min) → Future state (5 min) → Process and next steps (5 min). Write out every question you plan to ask, plus 3-4 follow-up questions for each section.
steps.tips: A script is a guide, not a straitjacket. You won't follow it word-for-word in real life. But having it written forces you to think through the flow and ensures you don't forget critical qualification questions.

steps.title: Prepare a MEDDIC Scorecard
steps.description: Create a blank MEDDIC scorecard that you'll fill in during the role-play. For each letter (Metrics, Economic Buyer, Decision Criteria, Decision Process, Identify Pain, Champion), write the specific questions you'll ask to uncover that information.
steps.tips: Don't ask MEDDIC questions in order — weave them naturally into the conversation. 'Who else is involved in evaluating this?' (Decision Process) can come up organically after discussing pain.

steps.title: Role-Play the Call
steps.description: Find a partner to play the buyer. Brief them on the scenario (but hold back some details so they can improvise). Run the full 30-minute discovery call. If possible, record it. The buyer should be realistic — give short answers sometimes, push back, mention a competitor, express time pressure.
steps.tips: If you don't have a partner, record yourself speaking both parts. Alternatively, use an AI tool like ChatGPT or Claude as a simulated buyer — give it the scenario brief and have a voice conversation.

steps.title: Self-Assessment
steps.description: After the role-play, fill in your MEDDIC scorecard based on what you learned. Then review the recording (or your notes) and assess: How much did you talk vs listen? Did you go deep enough on pain? Did you quantify the impact? Did you set clear next steps? Write 3 things you did well and 3 things to improve.
steps.tips: The talk-to-listen ratio is the single best indicator. If you talked more than 40% of the time, you're talking too much. Top discovery callers aim for 30/70.

deliverables: A one-page scenario brief (product, buyer persona, situation)
A full discovery call script with questions for each section
A blank MEDDIC scorecard with tailored questions for this scenario
A completed MEDDIC scorecard filled in after the role-play
A self-assessment with 3 strengths and 3 improvement areas

rubric.description: Questions are open-ended, layered (going deeper on answers), and designed to uncover real pain rather than confirm assumptions.
Pain is quantified with specific numbers, impact is explored across the business, and the cost of inaction is established.
The MEDDIC scorecard is substantially complete — at minimum, Pain, Economic Buyer, and Decision Process are identified.
The self-assessment is honest and specific — not generic ('I should listen more') but actionable ('I asked three leading questions in the pain section that I should rephrase as open-ended').
